text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Department of Medical Oncology

PATIENT: Margaret Okonkwo
ATTENDING PHYSICIAN: [Attending Oncologist]

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Routine oncology follow-up for metastatic clear cell renal cell carcinoma on cabozantinib. Patient presenting for scheduled restaging review and toxicity assessment at 9 months of cabozantinib therapy (approximately 18 months from initial diagnosis).

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Okonkwo is a 64-year-old woman of West African descent with a well-established history of stage IV clear cell renal cell carcinoma (ccRCC) of the right kidney with sarcomatoid differentiation (20%), right renal vein tumor thrombus, retroperitoneal lymph node involvement (3 of 5 nodes), and bilateral pulmonary metastases at diagnosis. She is now approximately 18 months out from her initial diagnosis and 9 months into second-line treatment with cabozantinib 40 mg orally daily.

To briefly summarize her oncologic history: She was diagnosed with stage IV (pT3b pN2 M1) ccRCC approximately 18 months ago after presenting with hematuria and flank discomfort. Tumor measured 11.7 cm at surgical pathology with ISUP/WHO grade 4 disease, sarcomatoid dedifferentiation in approximately 20% of the specimen, extensive coagulative necrosis, and lymphovascular invasion via the right renal vein. BAP1 loss was confirmed by IHC; tumor NGS (500+ gene panel, tumor-normal paired) revealed VHL p.R200* (pathogenic), BAP1 p.L572fs (pathogenic), PBRM1 splice site mutation, SETD2 p.R1625H (likely pathogenic), KDM5C p.Q690* (hemizygous), subclonal TP53 p.R248W (VAF 22%), CDKN2A homozygous deletion, and notably high-level MET amplification (copy number 7.2). MSI-stable; TMB 6.4 mut/Mb; PD-L1 CPS 14%. She underwent cytoreductive open right radical nephrectomy with retroperitoneal lymph node dissection without perioperative complications aside from prolonged ileus and acute-on-chronic kidney injury (eGFR nadir 38, recovering to 52 at 6-week post-op visit). She then received first-line Yervoy 1 mg/kg plus Opdivo 3 mg/kg every 3 weeks for 4 induction cycles, followed by Opdivo 480 mg every 4 weeks maintenance, based on CHECKMATE-214 data and the well-established activity of IO/IO doublets in sarcomatoid ccRCC and BAP1-deficient tumors. She achieved a partial response in pulmonary disease after 4 induction cycles (dominant right lower lobe nodule 1.7 cm → 0.9 cm) but subsequently developed radiographic progression at approximately 9 months into this regimen, with enlargement of pulmonary nodules (right lower lobe up to 2.4 cm) and new pelvic sidewall and right external iliac lymphadenopathy. She had clinical symptoms of dyspnea on exertion and new dry cough at that time. An MRI brain at the time of progression was negative for intracranial disease. She was then transitioned to cabozantinib 60 mg daily as second-line therapy, with the MET amplification providing additional biologic rationale. The starting dose was subsequently reduced to 40 mg daily after 4 weeks due to grade 2 palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia (bilateral palmar and plantar blistering, difficulty ambulating), which improved to grade 1 with dose reduction and intensive urea-based cream. She subsequently developed grade 2 diarrhea (4-6 loose stools/day) managed successfully with loperamide and dietary modification, and grade 2 hypertension managed with uptitration of amlodipine from 5 mg to 10 mg daily. The first restaging CT at 3 months on cabozantinib showed a meaningful response (right lower lobe nodule 2.4 cm → 1.9 cm; right pelvic sidewall node 1.9 cm → 1.4 cm; overall sum of target lesions decreased approximately 27% from pre-cabo baseline). Restaging at 6 months confirmed maintained stable disease with no new lesions. At the 6-month tumor board review, continued cabozantinib at 40 mg daily was endorsed given the MET amplification and absence of clinically available trial options; mild iron-deficiency anemia was identified (hemoglobin 10.8 g/dL) and oral ferrous sulfate alternate day dosing was initiated given her CKD. Today she presents for her 9-month restaging evaluation.

Regarding today's visit: Ms. Okonkwo reports overall feeling reasonably well. Her main ongoing concerns are fatigue and mild bilateral hand and foot sensitivity. She denies new or worsening shortness of breath and reports she is able to walk several blocks at a comfortable pace without significant dyspnea -- a meaningful improvement from the symptomatic progression she experienced on nivo maintenance approximately 9 months ago. She denies hemoptysis, chest pain, palpitations, or pleuritic pain. She denies bone pain, back pain, or new neurological symptoms such as headache, vision changes, or focal weakness. She has had no hematuria or new genitourinary symptoms from her remaining left kidney. Bowel habits are near-normal; she notes occasional loose stools (approximately 1-2 days per month) but is not requiring daily loperamide and this does not significantly impair her daily functioning. Appetite is preserved; weight is stable. She notes mild ankle edema at the end of the day, bilateral, which she has attributed to warm weather and prolonged sitting, though this will be examined. She denies any new skin changes, rash, or mucositis. She reports good energy in the mornings with afternoon fatigue that limits her to about 4-6 hours of productive activity daily. She continues to live at home independently with her husband, manages her own medications, and has been visiting with grandchildren, which she describes as motivating. She is continuing monthly meetings with the oncology social worker, which she finds supportive. She is adherent to cabozantinib 40 mg daily and takes her doses consistently in the evening with a light snack.

She denies missed doses of more than 1-2 days over the past 3 months (recalled once holding cabozantinib for 2 days around a family event due to gastrointestinal concerns, but otherwise consistent). She takes her levothyroxine in the morning, separated from cabozantinib by at least 4 hours, per prior counseling. She remains adherent to metformin 1000 mg twice daily and amlodipine 10 mg daily, and is taking ferrous sulfate 325 mg on alternate days. She reports that she takes calcium carbonate antacids occasionally for mild heartburn, and she was counseled previously about the potential interaction with levothyroxine absorption (spacing doses appropriately).

She has reviewed the results of today's restaging CT with imaging reviewed prior to the office visit by our team.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Fatigue grade 1, present, stable from last visit. No fever, chills, night sweats, or unintentional weight loss.
Pulmonary: Mild exertional dyspnea with sustained walking at pace (greater than 4-5 blocks), improved from prior. No cough today; denies hemoptysis or pleuritic chest pain.
Cardiovascular: No chest pain, palpitations, or syncope. Mild bilateral ankle edema at end of day. Blood pressure at home monitoring logs show consistent readings of 128-138/80-88 mmHg, well-controlled.
Gastrointestinal: Occasional loose stools, grade 1, approximately 1-2 days per month. No nausea, vomiting, or abdominal pain. No melena or hematochezia.
Genitourinary: No hematuria, dysuria, or urinary frequency changes. No symptoms related to the remaining left kidney.
Musculoskeletal: No new joint pain. Prior wrist and finger arthralgias (attributed to prior Opdivo, grade 1) resolved. Mild plantar sensitivity, grade 1, manageable.
Dermatological: Mild bilateral palmar and plantar erythema, grade 1 hand-foot syndrome, well-controlled with urea cream. No new rash.
Neurological: No headache, visual changes, focal weakness, numbness, or gait instability.
Endocrine: No symptoms of hypo- or hyperthyroidism. No polydipsia or polyuria beyond baseline.
Psychosocial: Reports mild anxiety related to upcoming scan results, otherwise mood is stable. Ongoing social work support.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

General: Well-appearing, well-nourished African woman in no acute distress. Alert and oriented x3. Appears stated age.
Vital Signs: Blood pressure 132/82 mmHg (right arm, seated), pulse 76 bpm and regular, respiratory rate 16 breaths/minute, temperature 98.2°F, oxygen saturation 97% on room air, weight 78.4 kg (stable from prior visit), BMI 28.2.
Head, Eyes, Ears, Nose, Throat: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Sclerae anicteric. Oropharynx without mucositis or ulcerations. No palpable cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy.
Chest/Pulmonary: Mild decreased breath sounds at the right lung base consistent with prior imaging findings (metastatic disease); no new focal findings. No wheezing or stridor. Comfortable respiratory effort at rest.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, gallops, or rubs. Mild 1+ pitting edema bilateral ankles, symmetrical. No lower extremity erythema or warmth to suggest DVT.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Right flank well-healed surgical scar from prior radical nephrectomy, intact. No palpable hepatosplenomegaly. No palpable abdominal masses. Normal bowel sounds.
Skin: Mild bilateral palmar erythema and slight hyperkeratosis at bilateral heels, consistent with grade 1 palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia. No active blistering. No new rash, petechiae, or ecchymoses.
Extremities: Full range of motion. No joint swelling.
Neurological: Alert. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Motor strength 5/5 in bilateral upper and lower extremities. Gait steady and unassisted. No focal deficits.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (REVIEWED TODAY)

Complete Blood Count:
- WBC 5.8 x10^9/L (normal)
- Hemoglobin 11.4 g/dL (stable from last visit, grade 1 anemia; prior nadir 10.8 g/dL)
- Hematocrit 34.2%
- Platelets 198 x10^9/L (normal)
- MCV 84 fL (normocytic)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium 138 mEq/L
- Potassium 4.2 mEq/L
- Bicarbonate 24 mEq/L
- BUN 26 mg/dL
- Creatinine 1.58 mg/dL (eGFR 45 mL/min/1.73m2, CKD stage 3a; stable from prior, pre-diagnosis baseline was eGFR 74)
- Glucose 108 mg/dL (fasting, well-controlled)
- Albumin 3.8 g/dL
- Total bilirubin 0.7 mg/dL
- AST 29 U/L (normal)
- ALT 32 U/L (normal; previously grade 1 elevated at 42 U/L, now resolved)
- Alkaline phosphatase 78 U/L (normal)
- Calcium 9.1 mg/dL (normal)
- Phosphorus 3.4 mg/dL (normal)
- Magnesium 1.9 mg/dL (normal)
- LDH 198 U/L (within normal limits)

Thyroid Function:
- TSH 2.4 mIU/L (normal; stable on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily)
- Free T4 not checked today (stable thyroid function)

HbA1c: 7.4% (well-controlled type 2 diabetes; stable)
Iron Studies: Ferritin 28 ng/mL (mildly low but improved from nadir of 14 ng/mL); TIBC 382 mcg/dL; serum iron 62 mcg/dL; transferrin saturation 16% (mild iron deficiency, improving with alternate-day ferrous sulfate supplementation)
Urinalysis: Trace protein (1+), no blood, no glucose, no casts -- consistent with CKD; no evidence of new nephrotoxicity or hematuria.

BP log reviewed (patient-maintained home readings): consistent 128-136/80-88 mmHg range over past 4 weeks.

---

IMAGING RESULTS

Restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast (obtained prior to today's visit, reviewed by our team and radiology):

The right lower lobe pulmonary nodule has decreased from 1.8 cm (as measured 3 months ago) to 1.5 cm. The right upper lobe nodule has decreased from 0.9 cm to 0.7 cm. The left lower lobe nodule remains stable at 1.1 cm. Small bilateral pulmonary nodules noted on prior scans appear stable or minimally smaller in aggregate. There is no new pulmonary lesion identified. The right pelvic sidewall lymph node, which had decreased from 1.9 cm (at progression) to 1.3 cm on prior restaging, remains stable at 1.3 cm today. The right external iliac lymph node has decreased slightly from 1.0 cm to 0.8 cm. The nephrectomy bed is clean without evidence of local recurrence. No hepatic lesions are seen. No adrenal lesions. No osseous lesions suspicious for metastatic disease. No new lymphadenopathy in mediastinum, retroperitoneum, or pelvis.

Overall impression: Continued stable disease with modest ongoing response in several pulmonary and nodal targets. Disease burden remains well-controlled on cabozantinib 40 mg daily. No new sites of metastatic involvement identified.

An annual MRI brain with and without gadolinium is planned per our surveillance protocol given the patient's history of prior brain-negative imaging at progression and ongoing systemic therapy for metastatic RCC -- this will be scheduled to follow today's visit.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. Metastatic clear cell RCC, pT3b pN2 M1, ISUP grade 4, 20% sarcomatoid differentiation, BAP1-deficient, MET-amplified -- currently on second-line cabozantinib 40 mg daily x 9 months:

Ms. Okonkwo continues to maintain excellent disease control on cabozantinib at the reduced dose of 40 mg daily. Today's restaging CT demonstrates continued stable disease with modest ongoing regression in several pulmonary nodules and the right external iliac lymph node. She has now been on cabozantinib for 9 months with no evidence of radiographic progression. This is gratifying given her high-risk tumor biology (grade 4, sarcomatoid dedifferentiation, BAP1 loss, MET amplification, TP53 subclonal mutation, CDKN2A deletion) and the fact that she progressed on first-line ipi/Opdivo at approximately 9 months into that regimen. The MET amplification identified on her tumor NGS continues to provide a biologically compelling rationale for cabozantinib, given its activity against MET in addition to VEGFR2 and AXL. We will continue cabozantinib 40 mg orally daily without dose modification at this time given good tolerability and ongoing disease control.

Plan: Continue cabozantinib 40 mg daily. Next restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis in 3 months. MRI brain surveillance to be scheduled (annual per protocol). Patient will contact the office for any new symptoms in the interim, particularly new or worsening dyspnea, chest pain, or neurological symptoms.

2. Palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia (hand-foot syndrome), grade 1, on cabozantinib:
Currently well-controlled at grade 1 with continued use of urea-based cream (40% urea) applied twice daily to palms and heels. No new blistering or ulceration. Patient instructed to continue current skin care regimen, wear well-cushioned footwear, avoid prolonged standing on hard surfaces, and report any worsening to grade 2 promptly. Podiatry follow-up as needed. No dose modification required at this time.

3. Diarrhea, grade 1 (sporadic), on cabozantinib:
Currently near-resolved with appropriate dietary management and as-needed loperamide. Patient using loperamide approximately 1-2 days per month only. Low-residue dietary habits reinforced. Patient advised to monitor stool frequency and report return to grade 2 (>4 stools/day above baseline). No dose modification needed.

4. Hypertension, grade 2 (resolved/controlled):
Blood pressure well-controlled on amlodipine 10 mg daily. Home BP logs reviewed and confirm consistent readings in the 128-136/80-88 mmHg range. Continue amlodipine 10 mg daily. No further dose modification of antihypertensive therapy needed at this time. Will continue to monitor BP at each visit.

5. Iron-deficiency anemia, grade 1 (improving):
Hemoglobin 11.4 g/dL, stable from last visit (improved from nadir 10.8 g/dL). Ferritin improved from 14 to 28 ng/mL on alternate-day ferrous sulfate 325 mg. Will continue current supplementation regimen. Recheck iron studies and CBC in 3 months. Given the patient's CKD stage 3a and the known suppressive effect of cabozantinib on hematopoiesis, anemia is expected to be multifactorial (CKD-related decreased EPO production, TKI effect, and prior iron deficiency); EPO level may be checked if hemoglobin declines further.

6. Chronic kidney disease, stage 3a (eGFR 45), on cabozantinib:
Creatinine 1.58 mg/dL, eGFR approximately 45 mL/min/1.73m2, stable compared to prior visits. She is now approximately one stage below her pre-diagnosis baseline (eGFR 74), reflecting the compounded effects of surgical nephrectomy, prior acute-on-CKD postoperatively, and ongoing TKI use. Continued monitoring at each visit; holding parameters for cabozantinib do not include mild CKD at this level. Encourage adequate hydration (2 liters water daily); avoid nephrotoxic agents (NSAIDs); reinforce metformin is safe at current eGFR but will require reassessment if eGFR falls below 30. Urology and/or nephrology referral deferred at this time given stable function. Urinalysis reviewed: trace protein consistent with CKD, no concerning new finding.

7. Hypothyroidism, immune-related, on levothyroxine:
TSH 2.4 mIU/L -- well-controlled on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. Continue current dose. Recheck TSH in 3 months. Patient reminded to take levothyroxine in the morning on an empty stomach, separated from calcium antacids and ferrous sulfate by at least 4 hours.

8. Type 2 diabetes mellitus on metformin:
HbA1c 7.4%, well-controlled. Continue metformin 1000 mg twice daily. Fasting glucose 108 mg/dL today. No significant hypoglycemic episodes reported. Reinforce dietary management. Continue annual ophthalmology and podiatry surveillance.

9. Psychosocial support:
Patient continues monthly meetings with the oncology social worker. She reports manageable anxiety around scan results, which is understandable given her disease trajectory. Quality of life is meaningful -- she is able to care for herself, participate in family activities, and maintain independence. Advanced care planning documentation is on file and was updated at the 6-month visit. Palliative care consultation was discussed today as a supportive resource available at any point, not only at end of life; patient acknowledged and expressed openness to considering this if symptom burden increases. No referral at this time per patient preference.

10. Ongoing disease monitoring and future considerations:
Given that Ms. Okonkwo is now 18 months from diagnosis with her disease remaining controlled on second-line cabozantinib, we have a meaningful window to consider what options might be available upon future progression. Her tumor molecular profile -- particularly the high-level MET amplification, CDKN2A deletion, and BAP1 loss -- may render her eligible for emerging clinical trials. We will continue monitoring the clinical trial landscape. At the time of her next progression, potential considerations would include enrollment in a trial evaluating MET-directed therapy, VEGFR/HIF-2alpha inhibitors (e.g., belzutifan given VHL loss), or combination strategies. Third-line options in this heavily pretreated context are limited by available evidence, and discussions of goals of care and quality of life will remain central.

Return to clinic in 3 months for restaging imaging review and follow-up assessment. Patient was given contact information for urgent concerns. She verbalized understanding of the plan and expressed gratitude for the multidisciplinary support she has received.

Time spent in face-to-face encounter: approximately 45 minutes, greater than 50% of which was counseling and coordination of care.

[Dictated and reviewed by: Attending Medical Oncologist]
